The retrieved documents collectively address the use of ultrasound-guided nerve blocks for postoperative pain management in total knee arthroplasty (TKA). One systematic review and meta-analysis of 30 RCTs found that adductor canal block (ACB) combined with local infiltration analgesia (LIA) provided superior pain relief and functional recovery compared to ACB alone [Doc 1]. A randomized trial demonstrated that a single-shot adductor canal block with liposomal bupivacaine reduced opioid consumption and pain scores compared to standard bupivacaine [Doc 2]. Another RCT showed that adding an iPACK block to ACB improved pain control and early mobilization compared to ACB alone [Doc 3]. A prospective cohort study reported that continuous adductor canal block provided better analgesia than single-shot block for the first 48 hours postoperatively [Doc 4]. One narrative review discussed general principles of regional anesthesia in TKA but did not provide original data [Doc 5].

The retrieved documents collectively indicate that the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in clinical decision support systems (CDSS) for diagnosing acute coronary syndrome (ACS) in emergency departments shows promise but is not yet established as standard practice. One systematic review [Doc 1] found that AI-based CDSS had a pooled sensitivity of 95% and specificity of 88% for ACS diagnosis, though it noted high heterogeneity across studies. A prospective cohort study [Doc 2] reported that an AI algorithm reduced the median time to diagnosis by 12 minutes compared to standard care. However, a randomized controlled trial [Doc 3] found no significant difference in 30-day major adverse cardiac events between AI-assisted and standard care pathways. The remaining documents address AI in CDSS for other conditions or general AI implementation barriers, not directly ACS diagnosis.
